coredata.dc:title: Early deployment of deep learning models for lithium-ion battery state-of-health prediction with limited initial data
coredata.dc:creator: Sim, Min-Sung
Noh, Jinhong
Yoon, Yong-Jin
coredata.dc:description: Data-driven predictions of the state of health (SOH) in lithium-ion batteries typically require large, high-quality datasets obtained through long-duration cycling experiments. This time-consuming process hinders the early deployment of SOH prediction models in practical applications. To address this challenge, we propose a learning pipeline designed for early model deployment with limited initial data. The pipeline incorporates a real-time fine-tuning loop that progressively improves the prediction performance as new data become available. In addition, we design a hybrid deep learning model, referred to as one-dimensional convolution layer–Transformer–long short-term memory (CTL-Net), to capture both local patterns and long-term nonlinear degradation behaviors. To compensate for limited data availability, a data augmentation technique is employed, enhancing the prediction performance. The initially deployed model achieves sufficient short-term accuracy, but the performance degrades over repeated cycles, and this is addressed through real-time fine-tuning. As additional cycling data become available, the model is fine-tuned in real time, enabling adaptive performance improvements without requiring full retraining. To validate the proposed approach under realistic conditions, initial training is conducted using experimental data from 24 battery cells (18,650 format), cycled at 3 C and 20 ± 1 °C. Fine-tuning with data from 23 additional cells yields high predictive accuracy (R 2 = 0.997) and reduces the root-mean-square error by 70% compared with the initially deployed model. Notably, the fine-tuned model outperforms a model trained once on all 47 cells, highlighting the advantage of continuous adaptation. Overall, this study demonstrates that the proposed pipeline enables both earlier model deployment and more accurate SOH prediction in practical battery monitoring scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
State of health
Early model deployment
Real-time fine-tuning
Adaptive performance improvement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Validation of the proposed learning pipeline
Performance of the proposed learning pipeline

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology
Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Innopolis Foundation through Technology Commercialization services, founded by Ministry of Science and ICT (2024-JB-RD-0010). J. Noh acknowledges support from the KAIST Jang Young Sil Fellow Program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the proposed learning pipeline. (a) Workflow of the methodology. (b) Architecture of the two-phase pipeline, including initial training and real-time fine-tuning. See Fig. 2 for the Conv1D–Transformer–LSTM network (CTL-Net).
Architecture of Conv1D–transformer–LSTM network (CTL-Net) demonstrating how the model tokenizes the SOH curve and input features to predict the SOH sequence.
Experimental setup for cycling tests: (a) schematic and (b) photograph of the HC5005P cycler (HNT, Korea) and temperature-controlled chamber.

SOH prediction performance of (a) the initially trained model and models after the (b) 12th, (c) 18th, and (d) 23rd updates. The blue solid and red dotted lines represent the measured and predicted SOH values, respectively (left axis). The orange lines with circles denote the error rates between them (right axis). The shaded region indicates the teacher-forcing range.

Comparison of SOH prediction performance between CTL-Net trained using the proposed learning pipeline and CTL-Net trained once on all 47 cells. The predicted SOH (dot–dot line) and the corresponding error rate (orange circles) from the proposed pipeline are the same as the results shown in Fig. 4d The predicted SOH (dash–dot line) and the error rate (green circles) are obtained from the CTL-Net trained on all available data without fine-tuning. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

Comparison of SOH prediction performance between the designed CTL-Net and other existing models: measured data (thin solid line); CTL-Net (thick solid line); transformer (dash–dash line); long short-term memory (LSTM, dot–dot–dash line); and gated recurrent unit (GRU, dot–dot line).

Distribution of real and augmented data: (a) principal component analysis (PCA) of the real data; (b) PCA of the augmented data; (c) t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE) of the real data; and (d) t-SNE of the augmented data.

Comparison of SOH prediction performance with different augmentation factors: measured data (thin solid line); augmented 0 × (dot–dot–dash line); augmented 1 × (dash–dash line); augmented 2 × (thick solid line); and augmented 5 × (dot–dot line).
Operating protocol for accelerated cycling tests. Here, CC and CV stand for constant-current and constant-voltage conditions, respectively.
Aging-stage-wise SOH prediction performance outcomes of the early-deployment model (Fig. 4a); Eqs. (11-14) express the definitions of MAE, RMSE, MAPE, and R 2.
Quantitative comparison of SOH prediction performance outcomes between the initially deployed model and sequentially fine-tuned models, based on the results in Fig. 4.
Quantitative performance of SOH predictions for the proposed CTL-Net and baseline models (Transformer, LSTM, and GRU), as shown in Fig. 6.
Quantitative performance of SOH prediction for the proposed CTL-Net across varying data augmentation ratios (0 ×, 1 ×, 2 ×, and 5 ×), as presented in Fig. 8.

head.ce:title: Early deployment of deep learning models for lithium-ion battery state-of-health prediction with limited initial data

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Daejeon 34141, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Daejeon 34141, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Nanyang Technological University (NTU), Singapore 639798, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Nanyang Technological University (NTU), Singapore 639798, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Data-driven predictions of the state of health (SOH) in lithium-ion batteries typically require large, high-quality datasets obtained through long-duration cycling experiments. This time-consuming process hinders the early deployment of SOH prediction models in practical applications. To address this challenge, we propose a learning pipeline designed for early model deployment with limited initial data. The pipeline incorporates a real-time fine-tuning loop that progressively improves the prediction performance as new data become available. In addition, we design a hybrid deep learning model, referred to as one-dimensional convolution layer–Transformer–long short-term memory (CTL-Net), to capture both local patterns and long-term nonlinear degradation behaviors. To compensate for limited data availability, a data augmentation technique is employed, enhancing the prediction performance. The initially deployed model achieves sufficient short-term accuracy, but the performance degrades over repeated cycles, and this is addressed through real-time fine-tuning. As additional cycling data become available, the model is fine-tuned in real time, enabling adaptive performance improvements without requiring full retraining. To validate the proposed approach under realistic conditions, initial training is conducted using experimental data from 24 battery cells (18,650 format), cycled at 3 C and 20 ± 1 °C. Fine-tuning with data from 23 additional cells yields high predictive accuracy (R 2 = 0.997) and reduces the root-mean-square error by 70% compared with the initially deployed model. Notably, the fine-tuned model outperforms a model trained once on all 47 cells, highlighting the advantage of continuous adaptation. Overall, this study demonstrates that the proposed pipeline enables both earlier model deployment and more accurate SOH prediction in practical battery monitoring scenarios.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries are central to modern energy storage systems due to their high energy density levels, long cycle lifetimes, and low self-discharge rates [1–3]. They are widely used in portable electronics, electric vehicles, and energy storage systems [4–8]. With the rapid growth of electric vehicles and renewable energy storage systems, research efforts have increasingly focused on optimizing battery performance and enhancing operational safety [9,10]. In this area, prior work includes studies of battery materials [11,12] as well as frameworks for optimizing source–load coordination and demand responses [13].

However, lithium-ion batteries continue to degrade, driven by repeated charge–discharge cycles, high C-rate operation, and temperature fluctuations, commonly leading to increased internal resistance and capacity degradation [14,15]. Because these stressors interact, battery aging often evolves in a nonlinear and multivariate manner, making SOH predictions difficult [16]. Robust SOH prognostics are therefore essential for maintaining long-term reliability and safety with regard to batteries [17,18]. In response, recent studies have proposed SOH prediction and monitoring methods for practical settings, including anomaly-aware health assessments [19], load-informed forecasting under varying operations [20], and continuous SOH monitoring frameworks [21,22].

In the literature, SOH predictions have been approached using physics-based models [23,24], empirical models [25,26], and data-driven models [27–29], with recent reviews providing broader overviews and comparisons [30–33]. Physics-based approaches describe battery behaviors using electrochemical models [34]. These models are often employed to develop filtering-based state estimators [35]. In these approaches, degradation-mode and mechanism analyses are widely used to link model parameters to degradation mechanisms [36,37]. In contrast, empirical approaches include SOH prediction methods based on features extracted from constant-voltage charging curves [38].

Data-driven models have gained attention for their ability to learn complex degradation patterns using neural networks [39–42]. Early machine learning approaches, such as Kalman filter extensions and support vector machines, achieved reasonable performance with small datasets [43]. However, these approaches often struggled with capturing nonlinear degradation trends [44], long-term prediction [45], and real-time adaptation [46].

More recently, deep learning models have been introduced to address these challenges. For example, models based on long short-term memory (LSTM) [47–49] or gated recurrent units (GRUs) [50,51] have been employed to capture sequential patterns, demonstrating strong performance in tracking battery degradation over time [52,53]. This performance is possible because the degradation curve reflects the macroscopic consequences of electrochemical aging, enabling deep learning models to learn embedded patterns and predict future degradation. Furthermore, Transformer-based models, which leverage self-attention mechanisms to learn global dependencies across long sequences more efficiently, have been proposed to improve long-term prediction performance capabilities [54–56]. Some studies have extended these deep learning models even further by integrating them into applications, such as electric vehicles, to validate their performance in real-world operational environments [57,58].

Deep learning-based SOH prediction models have shown promise in capturing complex degradation dynamics and enabling accurate long-term predictions [59]. However, their effectiveness depends on availability of large, high-quality datasets obtained through extended cycling experiments [60–62]. Accessibility to extensive data is essential for learning nonlinear degradation patterns and ensuring model robustness against measurement noise and cell-to-cell variability [63–65]. These experiments involve hundreds to thousands of charge–discharge cycles, with each cycle taking several hours to complete. As a result, collecting the necessary data can take several weeks or months.

This time-consuming process of data acquisition can hinder the timely deployment of SOH prediction models in real-world applications. Early access to system-level battery models is particularly important during the integration of batteries into systems under development, such as electric aircraft [66], drones [67], and wearable devices [68]. These systems often undergo short prototyping phases that include laboratory testing and pilot-scale field demonstrations. Thus, immediate insights into system reliability influenced by battery performance are necessary even when only limited data are available. In these cases, deploying SOH prediction models with limited initial data is critical for ensuring system reliability throughout rapid prototyping and evaluation.

To improve prediction performance in limited data settings, prior studies have explored various approaches. For example, data augmentation has been used to expand insufficient training data [69]. Fine-tuning has been applied to improve model performance with limited data [70]. However, although these approaches are effective initially, their performance can degrade over time because they lack a continuous update loop to incorporate newly collected data after deployment. To address this limitation, it is necessary to design an integrated framework that includes a continuous update loop to maintain reliable prediction performance after deployment.

In this study, we propose a learning pipeline for the early deployment of SOH prediction models with limited initial data. The key concept is to enable continuous performance improvement through a real-time fine-tuning loop that incrementally updates the model as new cycling data become available, without requiring full retraining. To support this approach, we introduce a hybrid deep learning model, Conv1D–Transformer–LSTM (CTL-Net), which combines a one-dimensional convolution layer, Transformer, and LSTM layers to effectively capture both local degradation patterns and long-term temporal dependencies. To further enhance SOH prediction performance, data augmentation is applied during both the initial training and fine-tuning stages to mitigate data insufficiency throughout the learning process.

This paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents the proposed learning pipeline, including the design of CTL-Net. Section 3 demonstrates the SOH prediction performance of the proposed pipeline with experimental cycling data. We analyze the improvements achieved through real-time fine-tuning and report an additional benefit—its performance exceeds that of a model trained once on all 47 cells. In addition, we investigate the effectiveness of CTL-Net and the effects of data augmentation on prediction performance. Finally, in Section 4, we conclude the study and discuss future implications of the proposed approach.

Fig. 1 shows an overview of the proposed learning pipeline, which consists of initial training and real-time fine-tuning. Limited sets of measured SOH degradation curves are first augmented with synthetic degradation patterns that reflect realistic cell behaviors (top-left panel of Fig. 1a). This augmentation expands the training dataset while preserving realistic degradation characteristics.

The CTL-Net, composed of convolutional, Transformer, and LSTM blocks (bottom-left panel of Fig. 1a), is then trained on both experimental and augmented datasets. This hybrid architecture is designed to capture both local patterns and long-term behaviors of nonlinear degradation. The detailed architecture and components of CTL-Net are described in Section 2.2.

In the early-deployment stage (middle panel of Fig. 1a), CTL-Net is provided with partial experimental data (up to 120 cycles in this study) and tasked with predicting subsequent cycles. To ensure stability in long-horizon predictions, a chunked autoregressive strategy is employed, where predicted values are recursively fed back into the model, while the sequence is segmented into short chunks for improved stability [71,72].

The early-deployed CTL-Net achieves high predictive accuracy in the short-term region, as indicated by the solid circle in the top-center panel of Fig. 1a, whereas prediction errors increase in the long-term region, highlighted by the dotted circle where the SOH declines rapidly. This increase in prediction error during the later cycles underlines the need for adaptive updates to enhance accuracy over the long term.

To this end, as new experimental data become available, these data are augmented in the same manner and used to fine-tune CTL-Net in real time (right panel of Fig. 1a). This real-time fine-tuning process progressively improves the model’s long-term prediction accuracy. This approach enables CTL-Net, in its early deployment, to provide accurate short-term predictions at the beginning of battery operation and, over time, improve long-term accuracy as more cycling data become available. Notably, unlike conventional models that require extensive data prior to deployment, our pipeline enables early deployment and improves performance outcomes through real-time fine-tuning.

Fig. 1b illustrates the two-phase pipeline. In the initial training phase, CTL-Net is trained on both experimental and augmented datasets and deployed for early use. In the real-time fine-tuning phase, newly obtained experimental data pass through a data gate that checks their suitability for training. Accepted data are then augmented and used to fine-tune CTL-Net. Through this iterative process, the model progressively adapts to ongoing degradation, thereby improving long-term prediction accuracy.

Fig. 2 presents the structure of the designed CTL-Net. It is composed of four main steps: a preprocessing module that converts the experimental SOH curve data and degradation features into input tokens; a Conv1D block that extracts local and short-term patterns; a Transformer that captures long-range and non-local dependencies; and LSTM layers that enhance temporal continuity between cycles.

The input to CTL-Net comprises three components: the battery index b , the cycle step t , and the feature sequence X , as shown in Fig. 2. The feature sequence is defined as
(1)
X =
{
x τ
}
τ = 1
N
where
N =
{
T
for
training ,
t
for
testing
.

In Eq. (1), x and T denote a feature vector and the total number of cycles in the experiments, respectively. To construct the feature vector
x τ
, three key features are extracted from the experimentally obtained SOH curve: (i) the initial capacity, defined as the discharge capacity measured at the first cycle, reflecting the baseline health of the cell; (ii) the initial slope of the SOH curve, computed as the average slope between cycles 1 and 50, capturing the early trend of degradation; and (iii) the cycle number at the knee-point, obtained by rescaling the cycle numbers to the same scale as the SOH values and identifying the cycle with the maximum deviation from a straight line connecting cycle 1 and cycle 400, representing the start of accelerated degradation. The SOH value at cycle step τ , together with these three key features, constitute the feature vector
x τ
.

The three input components are mapped into an input token at each cycle step, denoted as
e τ
. Specifically, the battery index b and the cycle step t are transformed into vectors through embedding layers, while the feature vector
x τ
is linearly projected into a latent space by a weight matrix. The resulting vectors are concatenated to form the input token
e τ
. Stacking all tokens over N cycles yields the input token sequence, defined as
E =
{
e τ
}
τ = 1
N
where N was given in Eq. (1). To incorporate the ordering of cycles, positional encoding is added to E , which is subsequently fed into the Conv1D block.

The operation of this Conv1D block is expressed as
(2)
c τ
= ReLU
(
W
conv
( 2 )
* ReLU
(
W
conv
( 1 )
*
[
e
τ − 1
,
e τ
,
e
τ + 1
]
+
b
conv
( 1 )
)
+
b
conv
( 2 )
)
,
τ ∈
[ 1 ,
N ]
,
where the input at the cycle step τ is a window of embeddings
[
e
τ − 1
,
e τ
,
e
τ + 1
]
, corresponding to the previous, current, and next cycle steps. In Eq. (2),
W
conv
( l )
and
b
conv
( l )
denote the weight matrix and bias term of the l -th convolutional layer (
l = 1 ,
2
), respectively. The resulting output
c τ
is a feature vector capturing the local features of the SOH curve around cycle step τ . During testing, the input token at the next cycle
e
t + 1
is replaced by a zero-padding vector.

Computing the outputs over N cycles yields the convolved sequence
C =
{
c τ
}
τ = 1
N
where N was given in Eq. (1). This sequence is fed into the Transformer encoder. This encoder effectively captures long-range relationships and temporal patterns through the self-attention mechanism.

The operator for this mechanism is defined as
(3)
Attention
(
Q ,
K ,
V
)
= softmax
(
Q
K
T
/
d
)
V
where Q , K , and V are the query, key, and value matrices, respectively, projected from the convolved sequence C . The product
Q
K
T
produces the attention score matrix, in which each entry indicates the similarity between the current cycle step and another step. For numerical stability, these scores are scaled by
d
, where d denotes the dimensionality of the key vectors. Applying the softmax function to the scaled score matrix produces attention weights. Multiplying this attention weight matrix with V produces the final output of the operation in Eq. (3). Each row of the result is a weighted sum of the value vectors, where the weights are determined by the similarity between the current step and the others.

The self-attention operation evaluates the individual contributions of the convolved vectors at all cycle steps to the representation of the current step. In recurrent models, information is propagated sequentially through intermediate states, which can make it difficult to capture long-range dependencies. In contrast, self-attention operates in parallel over the entire sequence, enabling it to quantify relationships between all steps, even those that are far apart.

To further enhance the representations, we employ the multi-head attention mechanism. In this approach, the convolved sequence C is projected into multiple subspaces through independent weight matrices. In each subspace, a separate self-attention operation is performed; each of these operations is referred to as a head. The i th head is defined as
(4)
hea
d i
= Attention
(
Q i
,
K i
,
V i
)
where
Q i
,
K i
, and
V i
are the query, key, and value matrices, respectively, obtained by projecting C with head-specific weight matrices. These projections are different between heads (i.e.,
Q i
≠
Q j
,
K i
≠
K j
, and
V i
≠
V j
for
i ≠ j
). The multi-head operator then concatenates the outputs from all r heads and applies a linear transformation with an output projection matrix
W O
, as follows:
(5)
MultiHead
( C )
= concat
[
hea
d i
]
i = 1
r
W O
.
This process enables CTL-Net to capture richer dependencies across the entire sequence.

The output from the multi-head attention is further processed by a position-wise feed-forward network, which is applied independently to each cycle step. This network consists of two linear transformations separated by a nonlinear activation function, enabling nonlinear mapping of the attended features. The resulting sequence from the Transformer encoder stack (see Fig. 2) is denoted by T . The encoded sequence is defined as
T =
{
t τ
}
τ = 1
N
, where each
t τ
represents the encoded vector at cycle step τ .

The encoded sequence T follows two different processing routes depending on the task: one for data augmentation and the other for prediction. The data augmentation setting is delineated first. In this case, T is fed into a two-layer LSTM network (dashed arrow in Fig. 2), which is employed to capture sequential dependencies across cycle steps through gating mechanisms that preserve long-term information. The LSTM network updates its hidden state
h
˜
τ
and cell state
c
˜
τ
at cycle step τ , as follows:
(6)
(
h
˜
τ
,
c
˜
τ
)
= LSTM
(
t τ
,
(
h
˜
τ − 1
,
c
˜
τ − 1
)
)
,
τ ∈
[ 1 ,
N ]
.

The resultant sequence is defined as
H
˜
=
{
h
˜
τ
}
τ = 1
N
, as illustrated in Fig. 2 for the data augmentation setting. The augmented SOH values are denoted as
y ˜
τ
at cycle step τ and obtained by a linear mapping of the hidden states such that
(7)
y ˜
τ
=
W out
h
˜
τ
+
b out
,
τ ∈
[ 1 ,
N ]
,
where
W out
and
b out
represent the weight matrix and bias vector of the output layer, respectively.

In the prediction setting, the encoded sequence T is processed through the Transformer decoder stack, as illustrated in Fig. 2 (solid arrow). The sequence of SOH values,
{
y τ
}
τ = 1
t
, is prepended with a start token at position
y 0
, and is passed through the SOH embedding layer, yielding
R =
{
r τ
}
τ = 1
t + 1
, where
r 1
corresponds to the start token. In mathematical terms,
(8)
r τ
= E
(
y
τ − 1
)
,
τ ∈
[ 1 ,
t + 1 ]
,
where E represents the embedding transformation. The embedded sequence R is then processed by the masked multi-head attention [73], producing the decoded sequence
S =
{
s τ
}
τ = 1
t + 1
. This decoded sequence interacts with the encoder output T through the encoder–decoder attention module and is further processed by the feed-forward network.

The resulting hidden representation
z
t + 1
is fed into a two-layer LSTM to capture temporal dependencies, as follows:
(9)
(
h
t + 1
,
c
t + 1
)
= LSTM
(
z
t + 1
,
(
h t
,
c t
)
)
.
Herein, h and c denote the hidden and cell states of the LSTM unit, respectively. The hidden state
h
t + 1
is then projected through the output layer to yield the predicted SOH value, as follows:
(10)
y ^
t + 1
=
W out
h
t + 1
+
b out
.

Finally, the prediction
y ^
t + 1
is compared with the ground truth
y
t + 1
, and the model parameters are updated via backpropagation. The training hyperparameters used for the initial training and the real-time fine-tuning process are summarized in the supplementary materials (Table A1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the proposed learning pipeline
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we validate the proposed learning pipeline using experimentally obtained data. Section 3.1 details the preparation of the experimental datasets used for the evaluation, Section 3.2 presents the performance of the proposed algorithm for SOH prediction, and Section 3.3 demonstrates the effectiveness of CTL-Net architecture by comparing it with existing models, in this case Transformer, LSTM, and GRU. Lastly, Section 3.4 describes a parametric study that assesses the effects of data augmentation on the prediction performance.

As shown in Fig. 3 , cell-cycling tests were conducted using an HC5005P cycler (HNT, Korea) to prepare datasets for model training and evaluation. The test cells were Samsung SDI lithium-ion batteries (ICR18650–26 J). Each charge–discharge cycle followed the protocol summarized in Table 1 , which consisted of constant-current (CC) charging at 3 C, constant-voltage (CV) charging at 4.2 V until the current dropped below 250 mA, a 30-min rest period to ensure electrochemical equilibrium inside the battery, and CC discharging at 3 C down to 2.75 V. All experiments were performed in a temperature-controlled chamber maintained at 20 ± 1 °C. In total, 48 cells were cycled for up to 400 cycles, which means that
T = 400
in Eq. (1).

From the experimental results, the SOH values were estimated as the discharge capacity at each cycle normalized with respect to the nominal capacity. Data from the first 24 cells were used to train the initial model, where data augmentation with a duplication factor of
m = 2
was applied (see the left box in Fig. 1b). The remaining 24 cells were employed in the real-time fine-tuning loop, with 23 cells sequentially incorporated for model updates with data augmentation and the last cell reserved for final testing. That is,
n ∈ [ 1 , 23 ]
and
m = 2
in the right box in Fig. 1b In addition, the teacher-forcing length is denoted as f and set to 120 cycles. Prediction performance is evaluated in the autoregressive range (121–400 cycles).

To quantitatively evaluate the prediction performance of the proposed learning pipeline, four metrics were used: mean absolute error (MAE), root mean squared error (RMSE), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), and the coefficient of determination (R 2), as follows:
(11)
MAE =
1
T − f
∑
i = f + 1
T
|
y i
−
y ^
i
|
,
(12)
RMSE =
1
T − f
∑
i = f + 1
T
(
y i
−
y ^
i
)
2
,
(13)
MAPE =
1
T − f
∑
i = f + 1
T
|
y i
−
y ^
i
y i
|
,
(14)
R
2
= 1 −
∑
i = f + 1
T
(
y i
−
y ^
i
)
2
∑
i = f + 1
T
(
y i
−
y ¯
)
2
,
where
y ¯
is the mean value of the measured SOH values in the autoregressive range (
t ∈ ( f , T ]
).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the proposed learning pipeline
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 illustrates the SOH prediction performance of the proposed learning pipeline. The initially deployed model, trained on the first 24 cells and evaluated on the 25th cell, is shown in Fig. 4a. The results after the 12th, 18th, and 23rd fine-tuning updates are shown in Fig. 4b–d, respectively, corresponding to
n = 12 ,
18 ,
23
in Fig. 1b These evaluations were performed on the 37th, 43rd, and 48th cells, respectively, with the 48th cell reserved for the final test. In each panel, the error rate between the measured and predicted values is plotted on the right axis, and the shaded region denotes the teacher-forcing range. The SOH prediction performance for all n in the range 0–23, along with the corresponding error-rate trends, is provided in the supplementary material (Fig. A1).

Across cells, the measured SOH curve shows a similar trend. In the early stage, SOH decreases gradually, with the decline becoming more pronounced around approximately 200 cycles. However, the early-stage degradation rates and the knee-point cycles are different between cells. To capture these differences, we use the key features extracted from the SOH curve, defined in Section 2.2, as model inputs.

The results in Fig. 4 highlight the limitations of the initially deployed model and the effectiveness of the proposed fine-tuning strategy. The initial model as shown in Fig. 4a provides accurate short-term predictions; however, its performance deteriorates rapidly as the cycle number increases. In particular, error accumulation becomes noticeable beyond approximately 300 cycles, leading to a steep rise in the error rate as the cycle number approaches 400. The prediction results for each aging stage of the early-deployment model are shown in Table 2 . This observation suggests that the initially trained model can be deployed in the early stage but requires adaptation to achieve long-term prediction accuracy.

As additional experimental data are incorporated through the proposed real-time fine-tuning loop, the long-term prediction accuracy adaptively improves. For instance, the long-term error is noticeably reduced after the 12th update (Fig. 4b), especially beyond 350 cycles. The performance continues to improve through the 18th and 23rd updates (Fig. 4c and 4d, respectively). After the final update, CTL-Net achieves the best prediction performance with the lowest average error rate (see Fig. A2 in the supplementary material).

The quantitative evaluation of SOH prediction performance is summarized in Table 3 . Compared with the initial model, the model after the final update achieves a 71 % decrease in MAE, a 70 % reduction in RMSE, and a 77 % decrease in MAPE, along with a 2.8 % increase in R 2.

These results demonstrate that the proposed pipeline significantly reduces the reliance on large initial datasets. This proves that sequential fine-tuning overcomes data insufficiency, enabling reliable long-term monitoring after early deployment.

Fig. 5 replicates the predicted SOH values and error rates of CTL-Net after the 23rd update (Fig. 4d). For comparison, we also trained CTL-Net using the datasets from all 47 cells in a single-stage training process without fine-tuning. Although both training approaches employ the same amount of training data, their prediction performance differs, as shown in Fig. 5.

This comparison highlights an additional advantage of the proposed learning pipeline. The prediction performance of CTL-Net trained with the proposed pipeline is superior, particularly in the long-term range beyond 300 cycles. In contrast, CTL-Net trained once on all 47 cells exhibits pronounced error accumulation, as shown in Fig. 5. This finding demonstrates that the proposed learning pipeline, which enables sequential fine-tuning and resultant adaptive updates, provides not only a practical solution for early model deployment with limited initial data but also a superior learning strategy compared with conventional single-stage training.

The performance of the designed CTL-Net is compared with three existing models that are commonly employed in battery SOH prediction research: Transformer, LSTM, and GRU. For a controlled comparison under the same problem setting, the learning pipeline in Fig. 1b is applied to each baseline by replacing the CTL-Net block in both the initial training stage and the real-time fine-tuning loop. The same training conditions are used, with a data augmentation factor of
m = 2
and the number of fine-tuning updates set to
n = 23
based on the same experimental datasets.

As shown in Fig. 6 , CTL-Net exhibits superior prediction accuracy compared with the other models. Although all methods follow the overall degradation trend, the Transformer, LSTM, and GRU models produce larger deviations from the measured SOH values, particularly in the long-term range, as highlighted in the inset of Fig. 6. In contrast, CTL-Net provides the closest match to the measured SOH values through the entire cycle range. This result demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed CTL-Net architecture for long-horizon SOH prediction.

Table 4 further confirms these observations, demonstrating that CTL-Net consistently outperforms the other models across all four quantitative metrics (MAE, RMSE, MAPE, and R 2). Therefore, the effectiveness of the proposed network design is quantitatively validated.

A data augmentation technique was employed in the proposed learning pipeline to compensate for limited data availability. Here, the effect of data augmentation on SOH prediction performance is investigated.

Fig. 7 compares the distributions of the real (Fig. 7a and c) and augmented datasets (Fig. 7b and d) using principal component analysis (PCA) and t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE). The PCA projects high-dimensional data onto linear axes ordered by variance, generating a low-dimensional representation that preserves the dominant variance structure. In addition, t-SNE performs nonlinear embedding by matching the pairwise distance-based probability distributions between high- and low-dimensional spaces while minimizing the Kullback–Leibler divergence.

As shown in the PCA (Fig. 7a and b) and t-SNE (Fig. 7c and d) projections, the synthetic data with an augmentation factor of
m = 2
closely follows the underlying structure of the real data. This confirms that the synthetic data can be regarded as reliable training samples.

Fig. 8 compares the SOH prediction performance of CTL-Net under different augmentation factors. The number of fine-tuning updates is set to
n = 23
, identical to the setting used in Fig. 6. Accordingly, the measured data and the 2 × augmentation result in Fig. 8 correspond to the measured data and CTL-Net curve shown in Fig. 6. The non-augmented setting (0 ×) shows the largest deviation from the measured data, whereas the augmented cases yield closer agreement, particularly in the long-term range, as highlighted in the inset.

These results demonstrate that data augmentation improves SOH prediction performance. The 1 × augmentation reduces long-term error compared with the non-augmented case, while the 2 × augmentation achieves the best performance among the presented cases. Notably, the 5 × augmentation leads to less effective improvements despite further enlarging the dataset, likely owing to redundancy or overfitting introduced by excessive synthetic data. Taken together with the quantitative results in Table 5 , these observations indicate that a 2 × augmentation factor provides the most effective balance between accuracy and dataset size. Thus, this augmentation factor was adopted in this study.

In this study, we have proposed a real-time fine-tuning pipeline for SOH predictions to enable the early deployment of an SOH prediction model even when the initially available training data are limited. The key component of this approach is a fine-tuning loop that adaptively updates the model as new experimental data are obtained, without requiring full retraining.

Because collecting SOH data through cycling experiments is inherently time-consuming, the early deployment of prediction models is often hindered. As demonstrated in this study, models deployed early can achieve satisfactory short-term accuracy but tend to accumulate errors over the long term. The proposed learning pipeline resolves this limitation. As the model can be deployed early with satisfactory short-term performance, its long-term performance subsequently is progressively adapted and improved as additional training data become available.

Another important component of this process is CTL-Net, designed to support adaptive performance improvements. As demonstrated in this study, CTL-Net outperforms commonly used models such as Transformer, LSTM, and GRU with regard to long-term SOH predictions.

Furthermore, the proposed fine-tuning pipeline yields superior predictive accuracy compared to CTL-Net trained once on the same datasets without fine-tuning. This finding highlights an additional advantage of the proposed adaptive pipeline, showing that continual fine-tuning delivers better long-term performance than single-stage training without updates.

This pipeline can support early SOH predictions during system integration and rapid prototyping, where battery-powered systems are evaluated through laboratory tests or pilot-scale demonstrations. It provides increasingly accurate predictions of battery health, thereby supporting system-level reliability assessments.

Min-Sung Sim: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jinhong Noh: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Yong-Jin Yoon: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Innopolis Foundation through Technology Commercialization services, founded by Ministry of Science and ICT (2024-JB-RD-0010). J. Noh acknowledges support from the KAIST Jang Young Sil Fellow Program.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.egyai.2026.100712.
